# Spatial Autocorrelation {#spatial_autocorrelation}

## Introduction

![](images/banner-autocorrelation.png)

### Aims {.unnumbered}

The aims of this practical are to:

1.  Understand why spatial autocorrelation needs to be considered when analysing spatial data.
2.  Understand the techniques we can use to assess for global and local spatial autocorrelation, including the underlying mathematics and assumptions.
3.  Test for spatial autocorrelation in ArcGIS and interpret outputs correctly.

### Application {.unnumbered}

To that end, we'll use county-level data from the [2024 US General election](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/election/2024/us/results) to investigate global and local spatial autocorrelation.

### Data {#autocorrelation_data .unnumbered}

The county-level data for this practical are available in [`data/us-census`] as follows:

-   US county geometries for 2023, mapped at a 1:500,000 scale [[Source](https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/GENZ2023/shp/)]
-   US county-level Presidential results for the 2024 elevation [[Source](https://github.com/tonmcg/US_County_Level_Election_Results_08-24)], which includes:
    -   Democratic vote total [`votes_dem`] and proportion (`per_dem`)
    -   Republican vote total [`votes_gop`] and proportion (`per_gop`)

*Note that other datasets are present in this directory and will be utilised later in the unit.*

### Tools {.unnumbered}

[Spatial Autocorrelation (Global Moran's I)](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/spatial-autocorrelation.html?tabs=dialog) (Spatial Statistics Tools), [Cluster and Outlier Analysis (Anselin Local Moran's I)](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/cluster-and-outlier-analysis-anselin-local-moran-s.html?tabs=dialog) (Spatial Statistics Tools), [High/Low Clustering (Getis-Ord General G)](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/high-low-clustering.html?tabs=dialog) (Spatial Statistics Tools), [Hot Spot Analysis (Getis-Ord Gi\*)](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/hot-spot-analysis.html?tabs=dialog) (Spatial Statistics Tools), [Project](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/project.html?tabs=dialog) (Data Management Tools)

------------------------------------------------------------------------

## Practical {#autocorrelation_prac}

[1] Open ArcGIS and load county geometry data [`cb_2023_us_county_500k.shp`, n=3235]:

*Note* some context [here](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/articles/cj4vn0qxy0lo). The county level is for administrative purposes, but interesting for looking at patterns. The actual state vote (which gives reps to the electoral college) is % based at the state-level.

[2] For simplicity, remove non-contiguous counties using Select By Attributes (STATE_NAME = Alaska, Hawaii, Puerto Rico, Guam, American Samoa, Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands, United States Virgin Islands), and either Delete Rows, or save selected to new features (Data -\> Export Features -\> Use the selected records -\> `cb_2023_us_county_500k_contig`)

STATE_NAME \<\> 'Alaska' And STATE_NAME \<\> 'Hawaii' And STATE_NAME \<\> 'Puerto Rico' And STATE_NAME \<\> 'Guam' And STATE_NAME \<\> 'American Samoa' And STATE_NAME \<\> 'Commonwealth of the Northern Mariana Islands' And STATE_NAME \<\> 'United States Virgin Islands'

[3] Use [Project](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/project.html?tabs=dialog) to create new feature layer a using a suitable projected CRS e.g., 102004 Lambert Conformal Conic, rather than the current geographic CRS (NAD83 - [EPSG:4269](https://epsg.io/4269)). This will cause issues if measuring geometries (e.g., areas, distances), as discussed in Understanding GIS (Week 2)

[4] Load in presidential results [`2024_US_County_Level_Presidential_Results.csv`] and join to geometries, using Joins and Relates -\> Add Join (`NAMELSAD`, `county_name`).

*Note* an alternative approach would be to use the Federal Information Processing Series ([FIPS](https://www.census.gov/library/reference/code-lists/ansi.html)) code, stored in `GEOID` and `county_fips` respectively, but different data formats (Text, Long) means we don't get any matches, and would have to do some more data processing.

[5] Inspect the Attribute Table for completeness and for key stats i.e., Explore Statistics of `per_gop`. Mean = 0.66% at a county level.

*Question*: Why does this not match the overall vote % [i.e., population-excluded]

-   gop_total = 81,382,360 (50.4% of total)
-   dem_total = 77,151,397 (47.8% of total)
-   gop+dem = 158,533,757
-   total_votes = 161,380,528

This is *close* to the reported [results](https://www.bbc.co.uk/news/election/2024/us/results), possibly due to removing non-contiguous states.

[Data caveat](https://github.com/tonmcg/US_County_Level_Election_Results_08-24):

"Although the data in this repository is extensive, it is not considered the authoritative source. Researchers are encouraged to verify specific results from primary data sources when needed"

[6] Modify symbology:

-   Vary symbology by attribute (per GOP), using 'Unclassed Colors'
-   Colour scheme = Red-Blue (continous)
-   Colour scheme \> Properties \> Reverse
-   Double click values, update 0-1, same for labels

*Note* Symbology using 'Unclassed Colors' doesn't always update correctly i.e., sometimes fails to show the data at all. **James** can you check this?

[7] Run [Spatial Autocorrelation (Global Moran's I)](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/spatial-autocorrelation.html?tabs=dialog):

-   `per_GOP` as input
-   Generate Report
-   Conceptualization of Spatial Relationships should be familiar following Practical 2, we'll use `Contiguity edges corners` here
-   **TO DO**:
    -   Discuss *row standardisation*
    -   Communicate the *underlying mathematics*, as interpretation is very easy with the html output

[8] Interpret initial output:

First, it's important that we look at any **warnings** or error messages that might arise. For this tool, we have some:

```{r, eval=FALSE, collapse=TRUE}       
WARNING 000642: Problems reading 1 of 3109 total records
WARNING 000848: Features with bad records (only includes first 30): us-county-contig.FID = 292.
```

If we check the Attribute Table, this is for the District of Colombia which has `<Null>` vote share. This is because of a mismatch between our geometry data (the District of Colombia as a single feature), and our presidential results table, whereas we have the vote share broken down at ward level (Ward 1 - Ward 8). We could either remove this geometry (Q: will this have a significant effect at our scale of analysis?) or we can add the vote share in manually.

```{r, eval=FALSE, collapse=TRUE}         
WARNING 000846: 4 feature(s) had no neighbors which generally invalidates the statistical properties of a test
WARNING 000847: Features with no neighbors (only includes first 30): us-county-contig.FID = 426, 534, 2018, 2616.
```

If we check the Attribute Table, this is for four counties with no direct borders:

-   Nantucket County (25019)
-   Dukes County (25007)
-   Richmond County (36085)
-   San Juan (53055)

*Note* these have no borders when using `Contiguity edges corners`, but likely would be valid features using a distance-based approach, so we can keep them for now.

The next step is to address the source of those warnings.

[9] Add results for the District of Columbia [[Source](https://tonmcg.github.io/US_County_Level_Election_Results_08-24/)]:

```         
District of Columbia,11001,District of Columbia,21076,294185,315261,-273109,0.069,0.9331,-0.8641
```
> Open `2024_US_County_Level_Presidential_Results.csv` in Excel, create a new row, copy the comma-delimited data above into the first column and split into the relevant columns using `Data` → `Text to Columns` &rarr; `Delimited` &rarr; `Comma` &rarr; `Finish`, then save the csv. 

*Note* because we used a dynamic table join before, rather than [Join Field](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/data-management/join-field.html?tabs=dialog), District of Columbia should now have data!

[10] Re-run [Spatial Autocorrelation (Global Moran's I)](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/spatial-autocorrelation.html?tabs=dialog) and inspect outputs:

- Warnings addressed in [9] no longer present
- Moran's Index	0.288339
- Expected Index	-0.000322
- Variance	0.000116
- z-score	26.814927
- p-value	0.000000
- Clustered: Given the z-score of 26.814927, there is a less than 1% likelihood that this clustered pattern could be the result of random chance.

[11] We can also exclude those non-neighbouring polygons using Select by Attributes (!= FIPS code), and re-running [Spatial Autocorrelation (Global Moran's I)](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/spatial-autocorrelation.html?tabs=dialog) using 'Use the selected records' (N=3105)

uscountycontig_Project.GEOID <> '25019' And uscountycontig_Project.GEOID <> '25007' And uscountycontig_Project.GEOID <> '36085' And uscountycontig_Project.GEOID <> '53055'

- No warnings at all (000846, 000847) as we've excluded those features
- The results have changed, but not meaningfully
- Moran's Index	0.289864
- Expected Index	-0.000322
- Variance	0.000116
- z-score	26.956676
- p-value	0.000000

[12] Run [Cluster and Outlier Analysis (Anselin Local Moran's I)](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/cluster-and-outlier-analysis-anselin-local-moran-s.html?tabs=dialog) for the full dataset (N=3109)

- **TO DO**:
    - Discuss *False Discovery Rate (FDR) correction*
    - Investigate *Number of Permutations*

[12] Interpet Anselin Local Moran's I output

- Map interpretation: vote clusters and outliers?
- Histogram
- **Moran's Scatter Plot**
- Statistics in attribute table e.g., SpatialLag RS

[13] Run with a different spatial relationships e.g., inverse distance squared 

*Note* we can use a distance-based measured here with a reasonable degree of confidence, unlike geographic coordinates, but **distortion** still important

*Note* Distance Band or Threshold Distance will use the units specified in the CRS (metres)

  [Q] How have the results changed? Why? When using inverse-distance squared, we have much larger areas of HH and LL, presumably because we have a much less conservative neighbourhood rule (unlike contiguity only)

[14] Run [High/Low Clustering (Getis-Ord General G)](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/high-low-clustering.html?tabs=dialog):

- Observed General G	0.000328
- Expected General G	0.000321
- Variance	0.000000
- z-score	13.624777
- p-value	0.000000
- Given the z-score of 13.62477738651207, there is a less than 1% likelihood that this high-clustered pattern could be the result of random chance.
- Discuss how the conceptual and mathematical approach **differs** from Global Moran's *I*.

[15] Run [Hot Spot Analysis (Getis-Ord Gi\*)](https://doc.esri.com/en/arcgis-pro/latest/tool-reference/spatial-statistics/hot-spot-analysis.html?tabs=dialog):

- Same inputs as Local Moran's *I*
- **TO DO**:
    - Reiterate *row standardisation* and *FDR correction*
    - Discuss the *Self Potential Field*
    - Communicate the *underlying mathematics*, as interpretation is very easy with the html output
    

[16] By the end of the practical, you be able to:

- Understand how we might assess for autocorrelation and hotspots
- Understand and demonstrate the importance of the spatial weights matrix
- Understand the difference between the tools (global vs. local) and the mathematical approaches

## Extra

As a comparison, we can run with 2008 election results (Obama vs. McCain), stored in `2008_US_County_Level_Presidential_Results.csv`

Link the results to the county geometry data (Add Join) and re-test for global and local spatial autocorrelation.

*Question*: How have the results changed over that 16 year period? Is voter spatial autocorrelation greater, less, or about the same and what does this reveal?

*Question*: What is a limitation of our current approach? *Hint*: Sort the `gop_per` column for the 2008 results. 

Answer: the county geometries / names / FIPS codes might have changed in this period so we have 80 counties of 3109 where there is no matching name. Instead, we should really use time-accurate geometries, available [here](https://www2.census.gov/geo/tiger/).

## Resources

-   Moran, P.A.P. ([1948](https://www.jstor.org/stable/2983777)). The Interpretation of Statistical Maps. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society, B 10: 243–51.
-   Moran, P.A.P. ([1950](https://doi.org/10.2307/2332142)). Notes on continuous stochastic phenomena. Biometrika, 37(1/2), pp.17-23.
-   Anselin, L. ([1995](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1538-4632.1995.tb00338.x)). Local Indicators of Spatial Association—LISA. Geographical Analysis 27, 93–115.
-   Anselin, L ([1996](https://www.taylorfrancis.com/chapters/edit/10.1201/9780203739051-8/moran-scatterplot-esda-tool-assess-local-instability-spatial-association-luc-anselin)). “The Moran Scatterplot as an ESDA Tool to Assess Local Instability in Spatial Association.” In Spatial Analytical Perspectives on GIS in Environmental and Socio-Economic Sciences, edited by Manfred Fischer, Henk Scholten, and David Unwin, 111–25. London: Taylor; Francis.
-   Getis, A. and Ord, J.K. ([1992](https://doi.org/10.1111/j.1538-4632.1992.tb00261.x)). The analysis of spatial association by use of distance statistics. Geographical analysis, 24(3), pp.189-206.
